chunks.text: The Connection Between Mouth Breathing and Sleep Apnea Mouth breathing is more than just a habit — it often indicates that nasal breathing is obstructed or difficult, which can disrupt natural facial and jaw development over time. When the mouth stays open to compensate for restricted airflow through the nose, the tongue sits low in the mouth instead of supporting the upper jaw. This can result in narrow arches, crowded teeth, and even changes in facial appearance.
Signs That You or Your Child May Benefit from Airway Orthodontics Airway-related issues often go undiagnosed for years, even though they can have a serious impact on your health.

Dr. Richard Hadavand offers personalized treatment plans designed to optimize both your smile and your health. What Is Airway Orthodontics. Airway orthodontics is a holistic approach to orthodontic care that addresses underlying anatomical and functional issues that may impact the airway.

By guiding jaw development and expanding the airway early in life (or correcting issues in older patients) airway orthodontics can help resolve or reduce these health challenges. It is often used in conjunction with appliances like palatal expanders, growth-guidance devices, or Invisalign to widen the upper arch and encourage nasal breathing. This kind of treatment is especially beneficial when started during childhood while the jaws are still developing.

Instead of simply straightening teeth, this treatment considers how the shape and position of the jaws affect a patient’s ability to breathe through the nose and sleep properly. Many children and adults experience conditions such as mouth breathing, snoring, and obstructive sleep apnea that are linked to narrow dental arches, underdeveloped jaws, or improper tongue posture.

Mouth breathing also plays a key role in the development of sleep apnea. When the airway is too narrow or blocked, it can lead to interrupted breathing during sleep, causing the body to wake repeatedly throughout the night. Sleep apnea is linked to serious health issues, including high blood pressure, heart disease, and impaired cognitive function. Airway orthodontics aims to address these issues at their root by improving jaw position, supporting nasal breathing, and expanding the airway.

Common signs that may indicate the need for airway orthodontics include: Chronic mouth breathing Snoring or noisy breathing during sleep Teeth grinding at night Frequent waking or restless sleep Daytime sleepiness or difficulty focusing Crowded teeth or narrow dental arches A long face, recessed chin, or gummy smile If you or your child experiences any of these symptoms, Dr. Hadavand can evaluate your airway and jaw development to determine whether airway orthodontics is a suitable option.

How Do Palatal Expanders Work. A palatal expander is custom-made to fit over several top teeth in the back of the mouth. The device consists of two halves connected in the middle with a screw. Adjustments to the expander are made through this screw, gradually moving the two halves apart and, in turn, widening the palate. This adjustment process is typically carried out by the parents or the patients themselves, following specific instructions provided by their McKinney orthodontist.

Palatal expanders are orthodontic devices used to widen the upper jaw (the palate) to improve the fit of the upper and lower teeth together. They are most commonly used in children and adolescents while their jaw is still growing. The expander stretches the cartilage and bone of the palate, encouraging a wider, more optimal arch form. This treatment can prevent , reduce, or eliminate the conditions caused by a narrow jaw, such as crossbites, overcrowded teeth, and alignment issues.
